content:
Your AI Coding Agent Can Already Read Your .env File. These 13 Concepts Are the Only Thing Stopping It.

10 min read
1 hour ago

--

13 concrete guardrails that decide whether an AI agent is safe on production code, or one autonomous edit away from an incident.

TL;DR: An AI coding agent with terminal and filesystem access can, by default, read your .env, quietly loosen a failing test's assertion instead of fixing the bug, or reformat a prod config it decided was "in the way." None of that requires a bug in the model: a missing guardrail is enough on its own. This guide breaks down the 13 mechanisms that actually close those gaps, grouped into six layers, each with the exact failure it exists to stop.

The lie everyone believes about agent safety
That confusion is a symptom of a broken mental model that almost every vendor sells: agent safety as one knob, “trusted” or “not trusted,” on or off.
A house is safe because of five separate things working at once: a lock on the door, a fence around the yard, a camera at the porch, a light on a timer, a safe for what actually matters. None of those substitute for each other, and a house missing any one of them has a specific hole shaped exactly like the thing that’s missing.
An agent that can read your files, run your terminal, and edit your code has exactly that many independent failure surfaces: what it can see, what it’s told to do, how much it can do without asking, what happens after it’s done, and where your code physically travels to reach the model. A single “trust this agent” toggle is one lock with no fence, no camera, and no safe behind it.
Control means a set of locks, and each one stops a different burglar.
Ask a team that’s shipped agents on a real codebase and you’ll hear the same handful of near-misses: a test got quietly loosened instead of the bug getting fixed, a .env file ended up in the model's context window, a config in k8s/ got "helpfully" reformatted along the way.
None of these are model problems. They’re missing-lock problems. And the locks that fix them are the same 13 concepts, no matter which agent tool sits on top.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Learn the concepts once. Recognize them in every new agent tool forever.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

THE VISIBILITY LAYER (What the agent can see and touch)
1. Read Access Boundaries
The first question is what the agent even sees, not what it can do.
Every agent builds a context window by reading files. If a secret is readable, it can end up quoted in a prompt, a log, or a suggested diff without anyone asking it to.
The fix is a denylist that acts before context is built: the agent never sees the file, not even a filename. It lives at .explyt/tools/.readignore:
# .explyt/tools/.readignore (gitignore syntax)
.env
secrets/**
*.pemA file matched here doesn’t exist for the agent. Not for reading, not for editing, not for referencing in an answer.
If it can’t be read, it can’t leak.
2. Write Access Boundaries
Here’s how it actually happens: an agent refactoring a service notices the port mapping in docker-compose.yml sitting right there in its context window, decides it "looks inconsistent," and rewrites it in the same commit as the feature. Nothing in the diff review flags it as unusual, because nothing marked that file as off-limits in the first place.
A different problem: files the agent should see for context, but should never touch.
# .explyt/tools/.writeignore (gitignore syntax)
docker-compose.yml
k8s/**
AGENTS.mdSame syntax as .readignore, same location under .explyt/tools/, opposite effect: visible, not writable.
3. Edit Scope
Here’s the scenario that burns every team eventually:
Edit Scope restricts where changes can land to an explicit set of files and folders; the agent can still read the whole project for context.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

This is the textbook TDD setup: code is editable, tests are read-only, and the “just relax the assertion” shortcut disappears at the tool level, not the prompt level.
.writeignore is a permanent project policy you commit and share with the team; Edit Scope is scoped to one task: "for this ticket, only touch module X."
The agent can look at the test. It just physically can’t touch it.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

THE INSTRUCTION LAYER (What the agent is told to do)
4. Rules
Two engineers ask the same agent to write tests for the same kind of service on the same day. One gets given-when-then with clear sections. The other gets a flat list of asserts with no structure, because the “right” way to write a test lived only in whichever prompt happened to spell it out that morning.
Some decisions the agent makes aren’t blocked by a boundary at all: how tests get structured, which naming convention applies, what a commit message should look like. Rules cover exactly that gap, matched automatically to files by a glob pattern.
One instruction, matched by pattern, followed by everyone on the team, not just whoever wrote the best prompt.
5. Project Config Files (AGENTS.md)
A new hire’s first PR takes three extra review rounds because the agent ran npm install in a repo that's used pnpm exclusively for two years, then quietly regenerated a lockfile nobody wanted touched.
AGENTS.md is a config file that’s always active, describing what a fresh teammate would otherwise have to be told on day one.
# AGENTS.md
Package manager: pnpm (never npm or yarn)
Test command: pnpm testRules:
- Never edit files under infra/ without asking first
- Functions max 40 lines
- Always write a test for new public methodsRules and AGENTS.md are not a firewall. The model reads them as text and weighs them against every other signal in the prompt: it usually follows them, but “usually” is not a guarantee. For a guarantee, you need the boundaries from the layer above. Config files shape intent; boundaries enforce limits.

THE AUTONOMY LAYER (How much the agent can do without asking)
6. Agent Modes
Not every task needs the same amount of trust. A code-review pass shouldn’t have the physical ability to edit a file, even by accident: that’s a stronger guarantee than “please don’t.”
A reviewer asks the agent “does this diff look right” and gets back an unrequested fix for a typo it noticed along the way, pushed straight to the branch. The review turned into an edit nobody asked for, because nothing stopped the agent from having the edit tool in hand to begin with.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Ask is read-only by construction: it can search, read, explain, with no terminal and no edit tool, full stop. Review keeps the terminal and build access, and can run IDE inspections, but it doesn’t hold the edit tool. The guarantee is that the write tool wasn’t in its hands to begin with, not that the agent chose not to write.
No edit tool in hand means no edit, ever, not even by accident.
7. Model Routing
The same premium model doing a real refactor at a steep per-token rate is also the one answering “which file is UserService.java in" a dozen times a day. That's the kind of lookup a cheaper, faster model handles in one tool call, at a fraction of the cost.
The fix is pinning cheaper, faster models to low-stakes subtasks, so the premium model is reserved for work that actually needs it.

THE REVIEW LAYER (What happens after the agent is done)
8. Diff-First Changes
An agent runs unattended overnight on a migration task and touches 40 files. By morning, the only way to know what actually changed is to trust its own summary, until someone opens a real diff and finds three unrelated files got “cleaned up” along the way.
Full autonomy still needs a checkpoint.
This is the last human checkpoint, and it should stay the last one no matter how much you trust the agent upstream, because trust is exactly what a bad edit erodes fastest.
9. Static Analysis Grounding
A diff review catches what a human notices by eye. It won’t reliably catch a SQL injection three call sites away from the parameter that introduced it: that’s a data-flow problem, not a “does this line look wrong” problem.
The fix is a deterministic analysis engine running on compiled sources, tracing tainted data from a source (an HTTP parameter) to a sink (a raw query) across function and class boundaries. It runs offline, and nothing leaves the machine. It doesn’t replace the diff review; it gives it something to point at, findings tied to a file and a line instead of a hunch.
The distinction that matters: an LLM-based review is probabilistic. It can miss something or misjudge severity. A data-flow scanner on compiled code is deterministic: it either found the tainted path or it didn’t, independent of whether the pattern would have caught a reviewer’s eye in a wall of diff text.
A finding tied to a file and a line beats a feeling about a diff.

THE EXTENSION LAYER (Third-party tools are also an attack surface)
10. Scoped Tool Config
A GitHub token set up once for a side project quietly stays active in every other repo you open on the same machine, including the one where you never meant to give an agent write access to Issues.
The fix is scoping the tool config to the project, so a token never survives the jump to the next repository.
11. Secret-Free Tool Config
A config file with a token pasted in as plaintext gets committed on a Friday afternoon. By Monday it’s sitting in the repo’s history permanently, whether or not anyone remembers to rotate it.
A config file checked into a repo is a config file other people read.
{
"env": { "GITHUB_TOKEN": "${env:GITHUB_TOKEN}" }
}The placeholder resolves from the environment at runtime. The repo never holds the secret.
12. Tool-Limit Isolation
An unglamorous but real failure: chat tool limits are finite, and a single well-connected MCP server can expose 60–70 tools on its own.
The fix that scales: once the combined tool count crosses the limit, each server gets wrapped as its own subagent. The parent agent keeps exactly one handle: “call this server.” The full tool list lives inside the subagent, invisible to the budget that matters upstream. Small servers under the limit skip the wrapping and run directly, no configuration required either way.
The tool list moved out of sight. The budget stayed intact.

THE DATA ROUTING LAYER (Where the code physically goes)
13. Model Provider Choice
Everything above controls what the agent does inside your project.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A team on a paid plan assumes “included model access” quietly means “our code helps train someone else’s model,” until someone actually reads the terms. On Explyt’s paid plans, the agent tool itself guarantees the opposite: your code isn’t stored and isn’t used to train any model. Beyond that guarantee, the model provider’s own policy is a separate layer, decided independently of the agent tool.
This typically takes three shapes:
→ Managed, vendor-hosted models: code and prompt travel through a controlled channel to the chosen provider, not used for training. → BYOK, your own API key: data goes straight to the provider under their policy, bypassing the tool vendor’s servers entirely. → Local / on-premise models: an enterprise deployment arranged directly with the vendor; code never leaves company infrastructure at all.
This layer connects back to the first one: if specific files must never reach a model regardless of which provider is chosen, that’s .readignore, decided before routing is even a question.
Every other lock in this list only matters after this one is decided.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The full picture
Visibility: → 1. Read Access Boundaries: the agent never sees the file → 2. Write Access Boundaries: sees it, can’t edit it → 3. Edit Scope: can edit, only inside an explicit set of files
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Instructions: → 4. Rules: glob-scoped conventions loaded automatically → 5. Project Config Files: always-on project
